    32â†’/-- The predicate that all (finite) subsets of `A` have distinct sums. -/
    33â†’def DistinctSubsetSums {M : Type*} [AddCommMonoid M] (A : Set M) : Prop :=
    34â†’  Set.Pairwise {X : Finset M | â†‘X âٹ† A} fun X Y => X.sum id â‰  Y.sum id

    38â†’  âˆ€ X âٹ† A, âˆ€ Y âٹ† A, X â‰  Y â†’ X.sum id â‰  Y.sum id

    66â†’of $\varphi$, so that $\varphi_1(n) = \varphi(n)$ and
    67â†’$\varphi_k(n) = \varphi(\varphi_{k-1}(n))$. Let $f(n) = \min\{k : \varphi_k(n) = 1\}$.

    69â†’$f(n)/\log(n)$ has a distribution function, i.e., for every real $c$, the natural density
    70â†’of $\{n \le N : f(n)/\log(n) \le c\}$ exists.

    73â†’Elliottâ€“Halberstam conjecture.

    19â†’/-!
    20â†’# Erdإ‘s Problem 320
    21â†’
    22â†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/320](https://www.erdosproblems.com/320)
    23â†’
    24â†’Let $S(N)$ count the number of distinct sums of the form $\sum_{n \in A} 1/n$
    25â†’for $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$. Estimate $S(N)$.
    26â†’
    27â†’## Known Bounds
    28â†’
    29â†’Bleicher and Erdإ‘s proved:
    30â†’* **Lower bound** [BlEr75]: $\log S(N) \geq (N/\log N)(\log 2 \cdot \prod_{i=3}^{k} \log_i N)$
    31â†’* **Upper bound** [BlEr76b]: $\log S(N) \leq (N/\log N)(\log_r N \cdot \prod_{i=3}^{r} \log_i N)$
    32â†’
    33â†’where $\log_i$ denotes the $i$-fold iterated logarithm.
    34â†’
    35â†’Bettin, Greniأ©, Molteni, and Sanna [BGMS25] improved the lower bound to:
    36â†’$$\log S(N) \geq (N/\log N)(2 \log 2 (1 - 3/(2 \log_k N)) \cdot \prod_{i=3}^{k} \log_i N)$$
    37â†’
    38â†’[BlEr75] Bleicher, M. N. and Erdإ‘s, P., *Denominators of unit fractions*, J. Number Theory (1975).
    39â†’
    40â†’[BlEr76b] Bleicher, M. N. and Erdإ‘s, P., *Denominators of unit fractions II*, Illinois J. Math. (1976).
    41â†’
    42â†’[BGMS25] Bettin, S., Greniأ©, L., Molteni, G., and Sanna, C. (2025).
    43â†’
    44â†’[ErGr80] Erdإ‘s, P. and Graham, R., *Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory*. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    45â†’-/
    46â†’
    47â†’open Filter
    48â†’
    49â†’open scoped Topology
    50â†’
    51â†’namespace Erdos320
    52â†’
    53â†’/-- The set of all unit-fraction subset sums from $\{1, \ldots, N\}$:
    54â†’$\left\{ \sum_{n \in A} 1/n : A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\} \right\}$ as a finite set of
    55â†’rationals. -/
    56â†’noncomputable def unitFractionSums (N : â„•) : Finset â„ڑ :=
    57â†’  (Finset.Icc 1 N).powerset.image
    58â†’    (fun A => A.sum (fun n => (1 : â„ڑ) / â†‘n))
    59â†’
    60â†’/-- $S(N)$ counts the number of distinct sums $\sum_{n \in A} 1/n$
    61â†’for $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$. -/
    62â†’noncomputable def erdos320_S (N : â„•) : â„• := (unitFractionSums N).card
    63â†’
    64â†’/--
    65â†’**Erdإ‘s Problem 320** [ErGr80, p. 43]:
    66â†’
    67â†’The ratio $\log S(N) / ((N / \log N) \cdot \log \log N)$ converges to a positive
    68â†’constant as $N \to \infty$. Equivalently, $\log S(N) \sim L \cdot (N / \log N) \cdot \log \log N$
    69â†’for some $L > 0$.
    70â†’-/
    71â†’@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    72â†’theorem erdos_320 :
    73â†’    âˆƒ L : â„‌, 0 < L âˆ§
    74â†’      Tendsto
    75â†’        (fun N : â„• => Real.log (erdos320_S N : â„‌) /
    76â†’          ((N : â„‌) / Real.log (N : â„‌) * Real.log (Real.log (N : â„‌))))
    77â†’        atTop (nhds L) := by
    78â†’  sorry
    79â†’
    80â†’end Erdos320

    34â†’exists some $k_n \ge 0$ such that $f^{(k_n)}(z) = 0$ for all $z \in S_n$.

    19â†’/-!
    20â†’# Is Every Convex Polyhedron Rupert?
    21â†’
    22â†’A polyhedron is Rupert if one can cut a hole in it and pass another
    23â†’copy of the same polyhedron through that hole.
    24â†’
    25â†’More formally: a convex body in â„‌آ³ is a compact, convex set with
    26â†’nonempty interior. A convex body X is said to be Rupert if there are
    27â†’two affine transforms Tâ‚پ, Tâ‚‚ âˆˆ SE(3) such that د€(Tâ‚پ(X)) âٹ†
    28â†’int(د€(Tâ‚‚(X))), where د€ : â„‌آ³ â†’ â„‌آ² is the evident projection, and int
    29â†’denotes topological interior.
    30â†’
    31â†’Not all convex bodies are Rupert. For example,
    32â†’- the unit ball is not Rupert
    33â†’- the circular cylinder of unit diameter and height
    34â†’  closed on each end by disks is not Rupert
    35â†’
    36â†’However, many convex polyhedra are Rupert. All Platonic solids, and
    37â†’most Archimedean and Catalan solids are known to be Rupert.
    38â†’
    39â†’Question: are all convex polyhedra with nonempty interior Rupert?
    40â†’
    41â†’*References:*
    42â†’
    43â†’* [Platonic Passages](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/314715434_Platonic_Passages),
    44â†’  R. P. Jerrard, J. E. Wetzel, and L. Yuan., Math. Mag., 90(2):87â€“98,
    45â†’  2017. conjectures ("with a certain hesitancy") that perhaps all
    46â†’  convex polyhedra are Rupert.
    47â†’
    48â†’* However, [An Algorithmic Approach to Rupert's Problem](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2112.13754#cite.JeWeYu17)
    49â†’  describes experimental evidence to suggest that three Archimedean
    50â†’  solids may not be Rupert.
    51â†’
    52â†’* [Optimizing for the Rupert property](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.00601)
    53â†’  is the source of some of the Catalan solid results, and has more
    54â†’  results for Johnson polyhedra as well.
    55â†’
    56â†’* [This video by David Renshaw](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=evKFok65t_E) visualizes
    57â†’  known results for Platonic, Archimedean, and Catalan solids.
    58â†’
    59â†’* This problem's name comes from the fact that it is a generalization
    60â†’  of [Prince Rupert's Cube](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prince_Rupert%27s_cube).
    61â†’
    62â†’-/
    63â†’
    64â†’namespace Rupert
    65â†’
    66â†’open scoped Matrix
    67â†’
    68â†’abbrev SO3 := Matrix.specialOrthogonalGroup (Fin 3) â„‌
    69â†’
    70â†’scoped notation "â„‌آ²" => Fin 2 â†’ â„‌
    71â†’scoped notation "â„‌آ³" => Fin 3 â†’ â„‌
    72â†’
    73â†’/--
    74â†’The result of transforming a subset of â„‌آ³ by a chosen rotation and offset,
    75â†’and then projected to â„‌آ².
    76â†’--/
    77â†’def transformed_shadow (X : Set â„‌آ³) (offset : â„‌آ²) (rotation : SO3) : Set â„‌آ² :=
    78â†’  (fun p â†¦ offset + (rotation *لµ¥ p) âˆک Fin.castSucc) '' X
    79â†’
    80â†’/--
    81â†’A convex polyhedron (given as a finite collection of vertices) is Rupert if
    82â†’there are two rotations in â„‌آ³ (called "inner" and "outer") and a translation in â„‌آ²
    83â†’such that the "inner shadow" (the projection to â„‌آ² of the inner rotation applied
    84â†’to the polyhedron, then translated) fits in the interior of the "outer shadow"
    85â†’(the projection to â„‌آ² of the outer rotation applied to the polyhedron)
    86â†’
    87â†’[Note: The restriction to (polyhedra determined by the convex hulls of)
    88â†’*finite* sets of vertices here is deliberate. Were we to generalize to
    89â†’arbitrary subsets of â„‌âپ؟ we'd probably want to make the containment
    90â†’relation more strict, e.g.
    91â†’  closure inner_shadow âٹ† interior outer_shadow
    92â†’to rule out, e.g. the open ball being Rupert. However, we didn't
    93â†’observe any such generalization in the literature yet, so we stuck to
    94â†’what was in the citations above.]
    95â†’-/
    96â†’def IsRupert (vertices : Finset â„‌آ³) : Prop :=
    97â†’   âˆƒ (inner_rotation : SO3) (inner_offset : â„‌آ²) (outer_rotation : SO3),
    98â†’   let inner_shadow := transformed_shadow (convexHull â„‌ vertices) inner_offset inner_rotation
    99â†’   let outer_shadow := transformed_shadow (convexHull â„‌ vertices) 0 outer_rotation
   100â†’   inner_shadow âٹ† interior outer_shadow
   101â†’
   102â†’/--
   103â†’Does the Rupert property hold for every convex polyhedron with nonempty interior?
   104â†’-/
   105â†’@[category research open, AMS 52]
   106â†’theorem is_every_convex_polyhedron_rupert :
   107â†’    answer(sorry) â†” âˆ€ (vertices : Finset â„‌آ³),
   108â†’       (interior (convexHull â„‌ vertices : Set â„‌آ³)).Nonempty â†’ IsRupert vertices := by
   109â†’ sorry
   110â†’

    29â†’/-- Points in the Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/

    32â†’/-- An affine subspace of $\mathbb{R}^2$ is a line if it has dimension $1$. -/
    33â†’noncomputable def IsLine (L : AffineSubspace â„‌ Point2) : Prop :=
    34â†’  Module.finrank â„‌ L.direction = 1

    36â†’/-- Two affine subspaces are parallel if they have the same direction. -/
    37â†’def AreParallel (Lâ‚پ Lâ‚‚ : AffineSubspace â„‌ Point2) : Prop :=

    53â†’    âˆƒ pâ‚پâ‚‚ pâ‚‚â‚ƒ pâ‚پâ‚ƒ : Point2,

    64â†’  آ· exact âں¨_, âں¨(Finset.range n).image Int.ofNat, by
    65â†’      rw [Finset.card_image_of_injective _ Int.ofNat_injective, Finset.card_range], rflâں©âں©
